description: A child showing a chair they repaired may be 'thoru', and so may the person who taught them. The word covers pride as satisfaction or dignity connected to one's own or another's achievement, identity, work, or relationship. It can be quiet self-respect or shared pride as readily as public celebration. Pride can stand beside other people's worth; comparison and 'thiku nila' (seeing as small) are separate acts.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip lightly against the upper teeth and let unvoiced air pass for 'th', then draw the tongue back and round the lips for stressed true-mid 'o'. Make a tap or short trill at the alveolar ridge for 'r', raise the back of the tongue, and finish with rounded high-back 'u'.
sound_symbolism: Breath passes the teeth into rounded 'o', then the brief tap lifts the word into a close rounded finish. The compact shape suits satisfaction held upright without needing a grand display.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Pride can be expressed without ranking anyone else below the speaker. The distinction keeps dignity available while contempt remains nameable as its own act.

semantic_domains.emotion: satisfaction or dignity connected to achievement, identity, or relationship
